content.text: Perfect! I have read all four files completely. Now let me compile a detailed summary of all the patterns found across these compound components. ## Detailed Summary of shadcn/ui Compound Component Patterns ### 1. **React Context Creation and Usage** **Carousel (Most Complex)** - Creates `CarouselContext` with type `CarouselContextProps | null` - Context includes: `carouselRef`, `api`, `scrollPrev`, `scrollNext`, `canScrollPrev`, `canScrollNext`, plus `CarouselProps` - Custom hook `useCarousel()` with error boundary (throws if context is null) - Provider wraps entire component tree at root level - Context values merged from hook state and component props **Tabs & Accordion** - Do NOT create their own context - they delegate to Radix UI primitives (`radix-ui/tabs`, `radix-ui/accordion`) - No custom context layer needed because Radix UI handles state management - These are primarily wrapper components with styling ### 2. **"use client" Placement** All three components place `"use client"` at the very top of the file (line 1), right before imports. This is necessary because: - Carousel uses React hooks and context (useState, useCallback, useContext, useEffect) - Tabs and Accordion use Radix UI which requires client-side interactivity ### 3. **Props Typing Pattern - React.ComponentProps** **Universal Pattern Used:** ```typescript React.ComponentProps ``` Examples: - Carousel main: `React.ComponentProps<"div"> & CarouselProps` - Carousel sub-components: `React.ComponentProps<"div">` - CarouselPrevious/Next: `React.ComponentProps` - Tabs root: `React.ComponentProps` - TabsList: `React.ComponentProps & VariantProps` - Accordion components: `React.ComponentProps` This pattern allows: - Full HTML attribute spreading with `{...props}` - Type-safe prop forwarding - IDE autocomplete for all native attributes - Extending with custom variant props when needed ### 4. **Data-Slot Attributes Usage** All components use `data-slot` attributes for semantic identification: **Carousel:** - `data-slot="carousel"` on root div - `data-slot="carousel-content"` on content wrapper - `data-slot="carousel-item"` on items - `data-slot="carousel-previous"` on prev button - `data-slot="carousel-next"` on next button **Tabs:** - `data-slot="tabs"` on root - `data-slot="tabs-list"` on list - `data-slot="tabs-trigger"` on triggers - `data-slot="tabs-content"` on content panels **Accordion:** - `data-slot="accordion"` on root - `data-slot="accordion-item"` on items - `data-slot="accordion-trigger"` on triggers - `data-slot="accordion-content"` on content Purpose: Allow CSS targeting and semantic debugging without colliding with user classNames ### 5. **Data-State and Data-Orientation Attributes** **Carousel:** - Uses `orientation` prop: `"horizontal" | "vertical"` - Passed to context for child components to consume - Used in `cn()` calls: `orientation === "horizontal" ? ... : ...` **Tabs:** - `data-orientation={orientation}` on root (passed from TabsPrimitive) - `data-variant={variant}` on TabsList - Tabs use Radix UI's built-in `data-state` and `data-orientation` attributes - CSS uses group selectors: `group-data-[orientation=horizontal]/tabs` - TabsTrigger uses: `data-[state=active]` pseudo-selector styling **Accordion:** - AccordionContent uses: `data-[state=closed]:animate-accordion-up` and `data-[state=open]:animate-accordion-down` - AccordionTrigger uses: `[&[data-state=open]>svg]:rotate-180` These attributes are provided by Radix UI primitives and enable data-driven styling without custom classes. ### 6. **cn() Usage for className Merging** **Pattern:** Always merge variants before user className ```typescript className={cn(baseClasses, conditionalClasses, className)} ``` **Carousel example:** ```typescript className={cn("relative", className)} className={cn("flex", orientation === "horizontal" ? "-ml-4" : "-mt-4 flex-col", className)} ``` **Tabs example:** ```typescript className={cn(tabsListVariants({ variant }), className)} ``` **Accordion example:** ```typescript className={cn("border-b last:border-b-0", className)} className={cn("flex flex-1 items-start justify-between gap-4 ...", className)} ``` **Rule:** User's `className` always comes last in `cn()` so it can override defaults. The `cn()` function itself (from utils.ts): ```typescript export function cn(...inputs: ClassValue[]) { return twMerge(clsx(inputs)) } ``` Uses `clsx` for falsy value handling and `twMerge` to resolve Tailwind conflicts. ### 7. **cva() (Class Variance Authority) Usage** **Only Tabs uses cva():** ```typescript const tabsListVariants = cva( "group/tabs-list inline-flex w-fit items-center justify-center rounded-lg p-[3px] text-muted-foreground ...", { variants: { variant: { default: "bg-muted", line: "gap-1 bg-transparent", }, }, defaultVariants: { variant: "default", }, } ) ``` Then used in TabsList: ```typescript className={cn(tabsListVariants({ variant }), className)} ``` **Why only Tabs?** - Carousel and Accordion use conditional ternaries for variants (simpler cases) - Tabs has multiple component variants and complex styling combinations - cva() excels when you need reusable variant composition ### 8. **Function Declaration Style** All components use **named function declarations** (NOT arrow functions): ```typescript function Carousel({ ... }) { ... } function CarouselContent({ ... }) { ... } function TabsList({ ... }) { ... } function Accordion({ ... }) { ... } ``` Benefits: - Named hoisting aids debugging stack traces - Shows component name in React DevTools - Slightly better code organization for compound components The only arrow functions are React hooks (`useCallback`, `useEffect`), not component definitions. ### 9. **Named Export Patterns** All exports use named exports at end of file: **Carousel:** ```typescript export { type CarouselApi, Carousel, CarouselContent, CarouselItem, CarouselPrevious, CarouselNext, } ``` **Tabs:** ```typescript export { Tabs, TabsList, TabsTrigger, TabsContent, tabsListVariants } ``` **Accordion:** ```typescript export { Accordion, AccordionItem, AccordionTrigger, AccordionContent } ``` **Pattern Details:** - Type exports are explicit: `type CarouselApi` - Component exports are named (no default export) - Variant objects exported for external use (tabsListVariants) - Grouped at file end for easy scanning ### 10. **Other Key Conventions** **Error Boundaries in Custom Hooks:** ```typescript function useCarousel() { const context = React.useContext(CarouselContext) if (!context) { throw new Error("useCarousel must be used within a ") } return context } ``` **Accessibility Attributes:** - Role attributes: `role="region"`, `role="group"` - ARIA: `aria-roledescription="carousel"`, `aria-roledescription="slide"` - Screen reader text: `Previous slide` **State Management Approach:** - Carousel: Manual with useState + useCallback + useEffect - Tabs/Accordion: Delegated to Radix UI (uncontrolled by default) **Component Composition:** - Each compound component is a "slot": root, trigger/list, content/item, etc. - Parent provides context, children consume it - Radix UI primitives handle low-level state/accessibility **API Pattern:** - Carousel exposes `setApi` callback to let parent control carousel imperatively - Exports type `CarouselApi` so TypeScript users can type their state ### Summary Table of Patterns | Pattern | Carousel | Tabs | Accordion | |---------|----------|------|-----------| | Context | Custom | None (Radix) | None (Radix) | | cva() | No | Yes | No | | data-slot | Yes (all) | Yes (all) | Yes (all) | | data-state | No (context) | Yes (Radix) | Yes (Radix) | | cn() | Extensive | Yes | Yes | | Function style | Named | Named | Named | | useClient | Yes | Yes | Yes | | Props typing | ComponentProps + custom | ComponentProps + VariantProps | ComponentProps | | Custom hook | useCarousel | None | None | | Radix dependency | No | Yes (TabsPrimitive) | Yes (AccordionPrimitive) |

## 1. SIDEBAR.TSX - Complex Compound with Context

**File**: `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775392774759-2z0637/apps/v4/registry/new-york-v4/ui/sidebar.tsx`

### Context Management
```typescript
type SidebarContextProps = {
  state: "expanded" | "collapsed"
  open: boolean
  setOpen: (open: boolean) => void
  openMobile: boolean
  setOpenMobile: (open: boolean) => void
  isMobile: boolean
  toggleSidebar: () => void
}
const SidebarContext = React.createContext<SidebarContextProps | null>(null)
```

### Key Patterns:
- **Provider as Root**: `SidebarProvider` wraps everything and provides context + `TooltipProvider` 
- **Custom Hook**: `useSidebar()` with error boundary (throws if context not found)
- **Cookie Persistence**: Saves sidebar state to `sidebar_state` cookie (7-day expiry)
- **useIsMobile Hook**: Uses custom hook to detect mobile breakpoint
- **Data-Slot Attributes**: Every component uses `data-slot="sidebar-*"` for styling hooks
  - Examples: `data-slot="sidebar-wrapper"`, `data-slot="sidebar-menu-button"`, `data-slot="sidebar-menu-action"`
- **Data-State/Data-Collapsible**: Parent state reflected in child styles
  - `data-state={state}` (expanded/collapsed)
  - `data-collapsible={state === "collapsed" ? collapsible : ""}`
  - `data-variant={variant}` (sidebar/floating/inset)
  - `data-side={side}` (left/right)
- **CSS Variables**: `--sidebar-width`, `--sidebar-width-icon` set at provider level
- **Keyboard Shortcuts**: Cmd/Ctrl+B to toggle sidebar
- **Composition Pattern**: 
  - `Sidebar` (main container, reads context)
  - `SidebarHeader/Content/Footer` (layout dividers)
  - `SidebarGroup/GroupLabel/GroupContent/GroupAction` (logical sections)
  - `SidebarMenu/MenuItem/MenuButton/MenuAction/MenuBadge` (nested menus)
  - `SidebarMenuSub/MenuSubItem/MenuSubButton` (submenu support)
  - `SidebarInput/Separator` (utility components)
- **Slot Component Usage**: Uses Radix `Slot` for `asChild` pattern in `SidebarGroupLabel`, `SidebarGroupAction`, `SidebarMenuButton`
- **CVA Variants**: `sidebarMenuButtonVariants` with variant (default/outline) and size (default/sm/lg)
- **Conditional Rendering**: Different rendering for mobile (Sheet) vs desktop (fixed positioned)
- **Rail Interaction**: `SidebarRail` for resize handle with cursor indicators

## Cross-Component Pattern Summary

### 1. **Data-Slot Pattern** (Universal)
- Every component has `data-slot="component-name"` attribute
- Enables CSS styling and DOM traversal
- Makes components debuggable and self-documenting

### 2. **Context vs No-Context**
- **Complex interactive** (Sidebar): Uses React Context with provider
- **Radix-based** (Tabs, Accordion, Dropdown): Leverage Radix's internal state management
- **Simple composition** (Breadcrumb, Pagination): No context, pure props

### 3. **Data-State/Data-Variant Management**
- `data-state="open|closed"` for binary states
- `data-variant="default|outline|destructive"` for style variants
- `data-[state=*]:` Tailwind selector pattern for state-based styling

### 4. **Orientation & Layout Variants**
- `data-orientation="horizontal|vertical"` passed through
- Uses `group/` prefixes to cascade styles down to children
- Responsive variants with `group-data-[orientation=vertical]/tabs:` selectors

### 5. **Slot Component Pattern**
- `asChild` boolean prop + `Slot.Root` from @radix-ui/slot
- Allows polymorphic component composition
- Example: `BreadcrumbLink` can render as `<a>` or custom component

### 6. **CVA (Class Variance Authority) Usage**
- Exported variants like `sidebarMenuButtonVariants`, `tabsListVariants`, `navigationMenuTriggerStyle`
- Handles variant + size combinations
- Cleaner than inline ternary styling

### 7. **Icon Integration**
- Icons from `lucide-react`
- Size standardization: `[&>svg]:size-4`
- Rotation/transform effects: `group-data-[state=open]:rotate-180`

### 8. **Portal & Z-Index**
- Portal pattern for modals/popovers (Alert Dialog, Dropdown Menu)
- `z-50` for overlays, prevents stacking context issues
- `data-[side=*]:` directional positioning

### 9. **Mobile Responsiveness**
- Custom hooks: `useIsMobile()` for breakpoint detection
- Conditional rendering: Different DOM for mobile vs desktop (Sidebar example)
- `hidden sm:block` for responsive text visibility (Pagination)

### 10. **Keyboard & Accessibility**
- ARIA labels: `aria-label`, `aria-current`, `aria-hidden`, `role`
- Keyboard shortcuts (Sidebar: Cmd/Ctrl+B)
- Focus management: `focus-visible:ring-[3px]` patterns

---

## Import Patterns

```typescript
// Always present
import * as React from "react"
import { cn } from "@/lib/utils"  // or "@/registry/new-york-v4/lib/utils"
import { Slot } from "radix-ui"  // For asChild pattern

// When needed
import { cva, type VariantProps } from "class-variance-authority"  // For variants
import * as [ComponentName]Primitive from "radix-ui"  // For Radix primitives
import { [IconName] } from "lucide-react"  // For icons
```

---

- **Checkbox/Radio Items**: Use `ItemIndicator` child for checked/selected state
  - `CheckIcon` for checkbox
  - `CircleIcon` for radio
- **Submenu Support**: `DropdownMenuSub` + `SubTrigger` + `SubContent`
  - SubTrigger shows `ChevronRightIcon` on right
- **Z-Index Management**: `z-50` for stacking context
- **Animation States**:
  - Slide in: `data-[side=bottom]:slide-in-from-top-2`, etc.
  - Zoom: `data-[state=open]:zoom-in-95`, `data-[state=closed]:zoom-out-95`
- **Extensive Component Composition**:
  ```
  DropdownMenu
    ├─ DropdownMenuTrigger
    └─ DropdownMenuPortal
         └─ DropdownMenuContent
              ├─ DropdownMenuGroup
              │  ├─ DropdownMenuLabel
              │  ├─ DropdownMenuItem
              │  ├─ DropdownMenuCheckboxItem
              │  └─ DropdownMenuRadioGroup
              │     └─ DropdownMenuRadioItem
              ├─ DropdownMenuSeparator
              └─ DropdownMenuSub
                 ├─ DropdownMenuSubTrigger
                 └─ DropdownMenuSubContent
  ```

---

## 7. NAVIGATION-MENU.TSX - Advanced Viewport Pattern

**File**: `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775392774759-2z0637/apps/v4/registry/new-york-v4/ui/navigation-menu.tsx`

### Key Patterns:
- **Radix Primitive**: `NavigationMenuPrimitive` from radix-ui
- **Data-Slot Attributes**: Complete tagging
  - `data-slot="navigation-menu"`, `"navigation-menu-list"`, `"navigation-menu-item"`
  - `"navigation-menu-trigger"`, `"navigation-menu-content"`
  - `"navigation-menu-link"`, `"navigation-menu-indicator"`, `"navigation-menu-viewport"`
- **Data-Viewport Boolean**: Root prop `viewport={true}` controls rendering of viewport component
  - When false, content uses portal instead
- **Data-State Attributes**: `data-[state=open]:`, `data-[state=closed]:`
- **Data-Motion Attributes**: `data-[motion=from-end]:`, `data-[motion=to-start]:`, etc.
  - Controls directional slide animations
- **CVA Export**: `navigationMenuTriggerStyle` exported for external use
- **Group Data Nesting**: Complex selectors like `group-data-[viewport=false]/navigation-menu:`
- **Viewport Mechanics**:
  - `NavigationMenuViewport` wraps `NavigationMenuPrimitive.Viewport`
  - Uses CSS custom props: `--radix-navigation-menu-viewport-height`, `--radix-navigation-menu-viewport-width`
  - Indicator positioned absolutely with rotation transform
- **Link Styling**: `NavigationMenuLink` with `data-[active=true]:` states
- **Trigger Icon**: `ChevronDownIcon` with rotation and `group-data-[state=open]:rotate-180`

---

## 8. ALERT-DIALOG.TSX - Modal with Compound Composition

**File**: `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775392774759-2z0637/apps/v4/registry/new-york-v4/ui/alert-dialog.tsx`

### Key Patterns:
- **Radix Primitive**: `AlertDialogPrimitive` from radix-ui
- **Data-Slot Attributes**: Complete identification
  - `data-slot="alert-dialog"`, `"alert-dialog-trigger"`, `"alert-dialog-portal"`
  - `"alert-dialog-overlay"`, `"alert-dialog-content"`, `"alert-dialog-header"`
  - `"alert-dialog-footer"`, `"alert-dialog-title"`, `"alert-dialog-description"`
  - `"alert-dialog-media"`, `"alert-dialog-action"`, `"alert-dialog-cancel"`
- **Data-Size Variant**: `data-size="default" | "sm"`
  - Responsive layout changes: `sm:` breakpoint conditionals
  - Footer: `group-data-[size=sm]/alert-dialog-content:grid` for grid layout
- **Data-State Animations**:
  - Overlay: `data-[state=closed]:fade-out-0`
  - Content: `data-[state=open]:zoom-in-95`
- **Portal Composition**: `AlertDialogContent` internally calls `AlertDialogPortal` + `AlertDialogOverlay`
- **Media Support**: Optional `AlertDialogMedia` slot for icons/images
  - Uses `:row-span-2` for layout in default size
- **Button Integration**: Action/Cancel buttons wrap Radix primitives with Button component
  - Default variant: "default" (Action), "outline" (Cancel)
  - Accepts size/variant props
- **Complex CSS Selectors**:
  - `has-data-[slot=alert-dialog-media]:` for conditional layout when media present
  - `sm:group-data-[size=default]/alert-dialog-content:` for responsive variants
- **Component Tree**:
  ```
  AlertDialog
    ├─ AlertDialogTrigger
    └─ AlertDialogContent (internally includes Portal + Overlay)
         ├─ AlertDialogHeader
         │  ├─ AlertDialogMedia (optional)
         │  ├─ AlertDialogTitle
         │  └─ AlertDialogDescription
         ├─ [Dialog Body Content]
         └─ AlertDialogFooter
              ├─ AlertDialogCancel
              └─ AlertDialogAction
  ```

---

## Cross-Component Pattern Summary

### 1. **Data-Slot Pattern** (Universal)
- Every component has `data-slot="component-name"` attribute
- Enables CSS styling and DOM traversal
- Makes components debuggable and self-documenting

### 2. **Context vs No-Context**
- **Complex interactive** (Sidebar): Uses React Context with provider
- **Radix-based** (Tabs, Accordion, Dropdown): Leverage Radix's internal state management
- **Simple composition** (Breadcrumb, Pagination): No context, pure props

### 3. **Data-State/Data-Variant Management**
- `data-state="open|closed"` for binary states
- `data-variant="default|outline|destructive"` for style variants
- `data-[state=*]:` Tailwind selector pattern for state-based styling

### 4. **Orientation & Layout Variants**
- `data-orientation="horizontal|vertical"` passed through
- Uses `group/` prefixes to cascade styles down to children
- Responsive variants with `group-data-[orientation=vertical]/tabs:` selectors

### 5. **Slot Component Pattern**
- `asChild` boolean prop + `Slot.Root` from @radix-ui/slot
- Allows polymorphic component composition
- Example: `BreadcrumbLink` can render as `<a>` or custom component

### 6. **CVA (Class Variance Authority) Usage**
- Exported variants like `sidebarMenuButtonVariants`, `tabsListVariants`, `navigationMenuTriggerStyle`
- Handles variant + size combinations
- Cleaner than inline ternary styling

### 7. **Icon Integration**
- Icons from `lucide-react`
- Size standardization: `[&>svg]:size-4`
- Rotation/transform effects: `group-data-[state=open]:rotate-180`

### 8. **Portal & Z-Index**
- Portal pattern for modals/popovers (Alert Dialog, Dropdown Menu)
- `z-50` for overlays, prevents stacking context issues
- `data-[side=*]:` directional positioning

### 9. **Mobile Responsiveness**
- Custom hooks: `useIsMobile()` for breakpoint detection
- Conditional rendering: Different DOM for mobile vs desktop (Sidebar example)
- `hidden sm:block` for responsive text visibility (Pagination)

### 10. **Keyboard & Accessibility**
- ARIA labels: `aria-label`, `aria-current`, `aria-hidden`, `role`
- Keyboard shortcuts (Sidebar: Cmd/Ctrl+B)
- Focus management: `focus-visible:ring-[3px]` patterns

---

## Import Patterns

```typescript
// Always present
import * as React from "react"
import { cn } from "@/lib/utils"  // or "@/registry/new-york-v4/lib/utils"
import { Slot } from "radix-ui"  // For asChild pattern

// When needed
import { cva, type VariantProps } from "class-variance-authority"  // For variants
import * as [ComponentName]Primitive from "radix-ui"  // For Radix primitives
import { [IconName] } from "lucide-react"  // For icons
```

---

This completes the exploration. The sidebar is the most architecturally sophisticated (custom context, state management, mobile responsiveness), while the others leverage Radix UI's primitives more directly with light styling wrappers.

Here is the complete, detailed implementation plan for the Stepper compound component. --- ## Stepper Component -- Detailed Implementation Plan ### 1. Files to Create | File | Purpose | |------|---------| | `apps/v4/registry/new-york-v4/ui/stepper.tsx` | The compound component source | | `apps/v4/registry/new-york-v4/examples/stepper-demo.tsx` | The interactive demo example | | (modify) `apps/v4/registry/new-york-v4/ui/_registry.ts` | Add stepper UI entry | | (modify) `apps/v4/registry/new-york-v4/examples/_registry.ts` | Add stepper-demo example entry | --- ### 2. Context Architecture Two React contexts are needed, following the exact pattern from `carousel.tsx` (createContext with null default, custom hook that throws on null). **StepperContext** -- provided by the `Stepper` root component: ```typescript type StepperContextProps = { activeStep: number orientation: "horizontal" | "vertical" } const StepperContext = React.createContext(null) function useStepper() { const context = React.useContext(StepperContext) if (!context) { throw new Error("useStepper must be used within a ") } return context } ``` **StepperItemContext** -- provided by each `StepperItem`, sharing the step number so children (StepperTrigger, StepperSeparator) can access it without requiring explicit props: ```typescript type StepperItemContextProps = { step: number state: "completed" | "active" | "pending" } const StepperItemContext = React.createContext(null) function useStepperItem() { const context = React.useContext(StepperItemContext) if (!context) { throw new Error("useStepperItem must be used within a ") } return context } ``` The `state` is derived inside `StepperItem` by comparing `step` with `activeStep` from `StepperContext`, then shared down through `StepperItemContext`. This avoids recomputing it in every child. --- ### 3. Component-by-Component Implementation #### 3.1 `Stepper` (root container) **Props type:** ```typescript type StepperProps = { activeStep: number orientation?: "horizontal" | "vertical" } ``` **Function signature** (matches carousel pattern): ```typescript function Stepper({ activeStep, orientation = "horizontal", className, children, ...props }: React.ComponentProps<"div"> & StepperProps) ``` **JSX structure:** ```tsx
{children}
``` **Key design notes:** - `group/stepper` enables Tailwind group selectors for children that need to adapt based on orientation at the group level. - `data-orientation` is set for CSS targeting, matching the Tabs pattern. - Horizontal layout uses `flex-row items-center`; vertical uses `flex-col`. - `gap-4` provides spacing between StepperItems (which include their separators). #### 3.2 `StepperItem` (per-step wrapper) **Props type:** ```typescript // step prop is required -- it identifies which step this item represents ``` **Function signature:** ```typescript function StepperItem({ step, className, children, ...props }: React.ComponentProps<"div"> & { step: number }) ``` **State derivation** (inside the function body): ```typescript const { activeStep, orientation } = useStepper() const state = step < activeStep ? "completed" : step === activeStep ? "active" : "pending" ``` **JSX structure:** ```tsx
{children}
``` **Key design notes:** - `group/stepper-item` enables child components to style based on parent `data-state` using `group-data-[state=completed]/stepper-item:` etc. - `flex-1 last:flex-initial` makes horizontal separators stretch between steps, while the final step (which has no trailing separator) does not stretch. - The `data-state` attribute enables both CSS targeting and child styling via `group-data-[state=*]`. #### 3.3 `StepperTrigger` (clickable circle/number) **Function signature:** ```typescript function StepperTrigger({ className, children, ...props }: React.ComponentProps<"button">) ``` **JSX structure:** ```tsx const { step, state } = useStepperItem() return ( ) ``` **Key design notes:** - Default content: displays the step number. When completed, shows a `CheckIcon` from `lucide-react`. When `children` is explicitly provided, it takes precedence. - Uses `size-8` (2rem / 32px) for the circle, matching the general button sizing in the library. - Three distinct visual states via direct conditional classes rather than CVA -- this is simpler for just three states and matches the carousel pattern (which also does not use CVA). - `shrink-0` ensures the circle never collapses in flex layouts. - `border-2` gives a visible outline for the active and pending states. - Uses semantic design tokens: `primary`, `primary-foreground`, `muted-foreground`, `background`, `ring`. #### 3.4 `StepperTitle` (label text) **Function signature:** ```typescript function StepperTitle({ className, ...props }: React.ComponentProps<"p">) ``` **JSX structure:** ```tsx return (

) ``` **Key design notes:** - Minimal component. No context consumption needed -- styling based on parent state can be done via `group-data-[state=*]/stepper-item:` selectors by the consumer if desired. - Matches the `text-sm font-medium` convention used across the library. #### 3.5 `StepperDescription` (description text) **Function signature:** ```typescript function StepperDescription({ className, ...props }: React.ComponentProps<"p">) ``` **JSX structure:** ```tsx return (

) ``` **Key design notes:** - Nearly identical to StepperTitle but uses `text-muted-foreground` for secondary text. #### 3.6 `StepperSeparator` (connecting line) **Function signature:** ```typescript function StepperSeparator({ className, ...props }: React.ComponentProps<"div">) ``` **JSX structure:** ```tsx const { orientation } = useStepper() const { state } = useStepperItem() return (

) ``` **Key design notes:** - The separator lives INSIDE the StepperItem it belongs to. When a step is "completed", its separator is also filled in -- this naturally creates the visual effect where the line between step N and step N+1 fills when step N is completed. The separator within StepperItem[step=N] is "completed" when `step < activeStep` (which is the same condition that makes the StepperItem itself "completed"). - Horizontal: thin horizontal line (`h-0.5`) that stretches with `flex-1`. This works because StepperItem has `flex-1`, so the separator inside takes remaining space after the trigger and text. - Vertical: thin vertical line (`w-0.5`) positioned with `ml-4` (offset to align with the center of the trigger circle) and `min-h-4` for minimum visible length. - `bg-primary` for completed, `bg-muted` for pending/active -- two-state visual only. #### 3.7 `StepperContent` (content panel) **Function signature:** ```typescript function StepperContent({ step, className, children, ...props }: React.ComponentProps<"div"> & { step: number }) ``` **JSX structure:** ```tsx const { activeStep } = useStepper() if (step !== activeStep) { return null } return (
{children}
) ``` **Key design notes:** - Conditionally renders -- only shows when `step === activeStep`. - Takes its own `step` prop (not from StepperItemContext) because StepperContent is typically placed OUTSIDE the StepperItem hierarchy, as a sibling to the step indicators. - Simple `mt-4` spacing. The consumer controls layout. --- ### 4. Complete File: `stepper.tsx` The file structure follows carousel.tsx exactly: ``` "use client" import * as React from "react" import { CheckIcon } from "lucide-react" import { cn } from "@/lib/utils" // --- Types --- type StepperContextProps = { ... } type StepperItemContextProps = { ... } // --- Contexts --- const StepperContext = React.createContext(null) const StepperItemContext = React.createContext(null) // --- Hooks --- function useStepper() { ... } function useStepperItem() { ... } // --- Components (named function declarations) --- function Stepper(...) { ... } function StepperItem(...) { ... } function StepperTrigger(...) { ... } function StepperTitle(...) { ... } function StepperDescription(...) { ... } function StepperSeparator(...) { ... } function StepperContent(...) { ... } // --- Exports (grouped at end, named only, no defaults) --- export { Stepper, StepperItem, StepperTrigger, StepperTitle, StepperDescription, StepperSeparator, StepperContent, useStepper, } ``` **Dependencies:** - `lucide-react` (for `CheckIcon`) -- already in the project, listed as a dependency in the registry entry. --- ### 5. Demo File: `stepper-demo.tsx` **Pattern:** default export function, `"use client"` directive for useState, imports from `@/registry/new-york-v4/ui/stepper` and `@/registry/new-york-v4/ui/button`. ```tsx "use client" import * as React from "react" import { Button } from "@/registry/new-york-v4/ui/button" import { Stepper, StepperContent, StepperDescription, StepperItem, StepperSeparator, StepperTitle, StepperTrigger, } from "@/registry/new-york-v4/ui/stepper" export default function StepperDemo() { const [activeStep, setActiveStep] = React.useState(0) return (

) } ``` **Important note about StepperContent placement:** In this demo, `StepperContent` is placed OUTSIDE the `` element. But `StepperContent` needs `useStepper()` to access `activeStep`. There are two ways to handle this: **Option A -- StepperContent outside Stepper (requires restructuring):** Move the context provider to wrap the entire outer div, which means splitting `Stepper` into a provider and a visual container. This is more complex. **Option B (recommended) -- StepperContent inside Stepper:** Place `StepperContent` as children of ``. The demo layout becomes: ```tsx {/* Step indicators */} ... ... ... {/* Content panels -- these need to break out of the flex row */} ``` But this creates a layout problem: the step indicators are in a flex row, and the content panels need to appear below. **Option C (simplest, recommended):** `StepperContent` does NOT use context. It simply takes `activeStep` as a prop directly: ```typescript function StepperContent({ step, activeStep, className, children, ...props }: React.ComponentProps<"div"> & { step: number; activeStep: number }) ``` This is the cleanest design because: 1. StepperContent can live anywhere in the tree. 2. No context dependency means no placement constraints. 3. The consumer already has `activeStep` in state. However, this deviates from the requirements which say StepperContent has only `step` as a custom prop. Let me reconcile this: **Final recommendation (Option D):** Keep StepperContent using context, but restructure the demo so everything is inside ``. Use a wrapper div to control layout: ```tsx
... ... ...
... ... ...
...
 ``` Wait -- this means the Stepper root div's flex direction would interfere. The simplest clean solution: make the Stepper root a plain div without flex, and let the consumer control layout. The flex behavior for the step indicators comes from an inner container. But that breaks the convention of Stepper being the flex container for the items. **Resolved approach:** The Stepper root should be a flex column, containing the step indicator row and the content below. The step indicator row is just the natural flow of StepperItems. Here is the refined Stepper implementation: ```tsx function Stepper({ activeStep, orientation = "horizontal", className, children, ...props }: React.ComponentProps<"div"> & StepperProps) { return (
{children}
) } ``` Then the step indicators are wrapped in an inner div by the consumer (or we could add a `StepperList` component, but that adds complexity). Since the requirements do not mention a `StepperList`, the simplest and most idiomatic approach is: the consumer wraps the `StepperItem` components in a layout div themselves. This is consistent with how Tabs works (TabsList is explicit). Actually, re-reading the requirements more carefully, the cleanest approach given the stated API surface is: **Stepper does NOT impose layout on its children. It is purely a context provider wrapping a div.** The demo uses utility classes to arrange children: ```tsx
... ... ...
... ... ...
``` This gives maximum flexibility. The Stepper root is `flex flex-col gap-4` by default, stacking the indicator row, content, and buttons vertically. --- ### 6. Registry Entries **UI Registry** (add to `apps/v4/registry/new-york-v4/ui/_registry.ts`): ```typescript { name: "stepper", type: "registry:ui", dependencies: ["lucide-react"], files: [ { path: "ui/stepper.tsx", type: "registry:ui", }, ], }, ``` Note: no `registryDependencies` needed since the stepper does not import other registry components. It only uses `cn` from utils (which is built-in) and `lucide-react` (npm dependency). **Examples Registry** (add to `apps/v4/registry/new-york-v4/examples/_registry.ts`): ```typescript { name: "stepper-demo", type: "registry:example", registryDependencies: ["stepper", "button"], files: [ { path: "examples/stepper-demo.tsx", type: "registry:example", }, ], }, ``` Both `stepper` and `button` are listed because the demo imports both. --- ### 7. Implementation Sequence 1. **Create `stepper.tsx`** -- the main component file. Implement in this order within the file: - `"use client"` directive - Imports (`React`, `CheckIcon`, `cn`) - Type definitions for both contexts - Both context objects - Both hooks (`useStepper`, `useStepperItem`) - Components: `Stepper`, `StepperItem`, `StepperTrigger`, `StepperTitle`, `StepperDescription`, `StepperSeparator`, `StepperContent` - Export block at end 2. **Add registry entry** in `ui/_registry.ts` -- insert alphabetically (after "spinner", before "switch" or wherever "stepper" falls alphabetically). 3. **Create `stepper-demo.tsx`** -- the example file. 4. **Add registry entry** in `examples/_registry.ts` -- insert alphabetically. --- ### 8. Potential Challenges and Edge Cases **Challenge 1: Separator in the last step.** The last StepperItem should NOT have a separator. This is handled by the consumer simply not including `` in the last step's JSX. This is the same pattern as carousel (where the consumer controls what goes inside each item). **Challenge 2: Vertical orientation layout.** In vertical mode, each StepperItem should stack with the separator running vertically between steps. The StepperItem's flex direction changes to column, and the separator uses width instead of height. The `ml-4` on the vertical separator aligns it with the center of the 32px (size-8) trigger circle (16px = ml-4). **Challenge 3: StepperContent outside StepperItem context.** `StepperContent` uses `useStepper()` (not `useStepperItem()`), so it reads `activeStep` from the Stepper-level context. It is placed as a sibling to StepperItems, not inside them. This is by design. **Challenge 4: Zero-indexed vs one-indexed steps.** The plan uses zero-indexed steps (0, 1, 2) which is the natural JavaScript convention. The demo shows step numbers starting from 1 in the UI (because `StepperTrigger` displays `step + 1`... actually no, it should display the raw step number). Let me clarify: the `step` prop IS the display number if the consumer wants 1-based steps. The requirements do not specify. For the demo, using `step={0}` means the trigger shows "0" by default, which is bad UX. **Resolution:** Use 1-based step numbers in the demo (`step={1}`, `step={2}`, `step={3}`) and set `activeStep` starting from 1. This is more natural for end users: ```tsx const [activeStep, setActiveStep] = React.useState(1) // ... // ... ``` **Challenge 5: Accessibility.** The stepper should have appropriate ARIA attributes. Here is the complete, detailed implementation plan for the Stepper compound component. --- ## Stepper Component -- Detailed Implementation Plan ### 1. Files to Create | File | Purpose | |------|---------| | `apps/v4/registry/new-york-v4/ui/stepper.tsx` | The compound component source | | `apps/v4/registry/new-york-v4/examples/stepper-demo.tsx` | The interactive demo example | | (modify) `apps/v4/registry/new-york-v4/ui/_registry.ts` | Add stepper UI entry | | (modify) `apps/v4/registry/new-york-v4/examples/_registry.ts` | Add stepper-demo example entry | --- ### 2. Context Architecture Two React contexts are needed, following the exact pattern from `carousel.tsx` (createContext with null default, custom hook that throws on null). **StepperContext** -- provided by the `Stepper` root component: ```typescript type StepperContextProps = { activeStep: number orientation: "horizontal" | "vertical" } const StepperContext = React.createContext(null) function useStepper() { const context = React.useContext(StepperContext) if (!context) { throw new Error("useStepper must be used within a ") } return context } ``` **StepperItemContext** -- provided by each `StepperItem`, sharing the step number so children (StepperTrigger, StepperSeparator) can access it without requiring explicit props: ```typescript type StepperItemContextProps = { step: number state: "completed" | "active" | "pending" } const StepperItemContext = React.createContext(null) function useStepperItem() { const context = React.useContext(StepperItemContext) if (!context) { throw new Error("useStepperItem must be used within a ") } return context } ``` The `state` is derived inside `StepperItem` by comparing `step` with `activeStep` from `StepperContext`, then shared down through `StepperItemContext`. This avoids recomputing it in every child. --- ### 3. Component-by-Component Implementation #### 3.1 `Stepper` (root container) **Props type:** ```typescript type StepperProps = { activeStep: number orientation?: "horizontal" | "vertical" } ``` **Function signature** (matches carousel pattern): ```typescript function Stepper({ activeStep, orientation = "horizontal", className, children, ...props }: React.ComponentProps<"div"> & StepperProps) ``` **JSX structure:** ```tsx
{children}
``` **Key design notes:** - `group/stepper` enables Tailwind group selectors for children that need to adapt based on orientation at the group level. - `data-orientation` is set for CSS targeting, matching the Tabs pattern. - Horizontal layout uses `flex-row items-center`; vertical uses `flex-col`. - `gap-4` provides spacing between StepperItems (which include their separators). #### 3.2 `StepperItem` (per-step wrapper) **Props type:** ```typescript // step prop is required -- it identifies which step this item represents ``` **Function signature:** ```typescript function StepperItem({ step, className, children, ...props }: React.ComponentProps<"div"> & { step: number }) ``` **State derivation** (inside the function body): ```typescript const { activeStep, orientation } = useStepper() const state = step < activeStep ? "completed" : step === activeStep ? "active" : "pending" ``` **JSX structure:** ```tsx
{children}
``` **Key design notes:** - `group/stepper-item` enables child components to style based on parent `data-state` using `group-data-[state=completed]/stepper-item:` etc. - `flex-1 last:flex-initial` makes horizontal separators stretch between steps, while the final step (which has no trailing separator) does not stretch. - The `data-state` attribute enables both CSS targeting and child styling via `group-data-[state=*]`. #### 3.3 `StepperTrigger` (clickable circle/number) **Function signature:** ```typescript function StepperTrigger({ className, children, ...props }: React.ComponentProps<"button">) ``` **JSX structure:** ```tsx const { step, state } = useStepperItem() return ( ) ``` **Key design notes:** - Default content: displays the step number. When completed, shows a `CheckIcon` from `lucide-react`. When `children` is explicitly provided, it takes precedence. - Uses `size-8` (2rem / 32px) for the circle, matching the general button sizing in the library. - Three distinct visual states via direct conditional classes rather than CVA -- this is simpler for just three states and matches the carousel pattern (which also does not use CVA). - `shrink-0` ensures the circle never collapses in flex layouts. - `border-2` gives a visible outline for the active and pending states. - Uses semantic design tokens: `primary`, `primary-foreground`, `muted-foreground`, `background`, `ring`. #### 3.4 `StepperTitle` (label text) **Function signature:** ```typescript function StepperTitle({ className, ...props }: React.ComponentProps<"p">) ``` **JSX structure:** ```tsx return (

) ``` **Key design notes:** - Minimal component. No context consumption needed -- styling based on parent state can be done via `group-data-[state=*]/stepper-item:` selectors by the consumer if desired. - Matches the `text-sm font-medium` convention used across the library. #### 3.5 `StepperDescription` (description text) **Function signature:** ```typescript function StepperDescription({ className, ...props }: React.ComponentProps<"p">) ``` **JSX structure:** ```tsx return (

) ``` **Key design notes:** - Nearly identical to StepperTitle but uses `text-muted-foreground` for secondary text. #### 3.6 `StepperSeparator` (connecting line) **Function signature:** ```typescript function StepperSeparator({ className, ...props }: React.ComponentProps<"div">) ``` **JSX structure:** ```tsx const { orientation } = useStepper() const { state } = useStepperItem() return (

) ``` **Key design notes:** - The separator lives INSIDE the StepperItem it belongs to. When a step is "completed", its separator is also filled in -- this naturally creates the visual effect where the line between step N and step N+1 fills when step N is completed. The separator within StepperItem[step=N] is "completed" when `step < activeStep` (which is the same condition that makes the StepperItem itself "completed"). - Horizontal: thin horizontal line (`h-0.5`) that stretches with `flex-1`. This works because StepperItem has `flex-1`, so the separator inside takes remaining space after the trigger and text. - Vertical: thin vertical line (`w-0.5`) positioned with `ml-4` (offset to align with the center of the trigger circle) and `min-h-4` for minimum visible length. - `bg-primary` for completed, `bg-muted` for pending/active -- two-state visual only. #### 3.7 `StepperContent` (content panel) **Function signature:** ```typescript function StepperContent({ step, className, children, ...props }: React.ComponentProps<"div"> & { step: number }) ``` **JSX structure:** ```tsx const { activeStep } = useStepper() if (step !== activeStep) { return null } return (
